questions.q: To which forest community did Ekalavya belong?
questions.explanation: Ekalavya was a prince of the Nishadas, a forest-dwelling community of hunters.

questions.q: Whom did Ekalavya revere as his guru even after being turned away?
questions.explanation: Though Drona refused to teach him, Ekalavya accepted Drona as his guru in his heart and trained in his name.

questions.q: Why did Drona refuse to accept Ekalavya as a pupil?
options: The boy had no bow of his own
Because of his low Nishada birth
He arrived after lessons had ended
He could not pay the teaching fee
questions.explanation: Drona declined the Nishada boy on account of his birth, mindful of his royal pupils and of Arjuna's promised pre-eminence.

questions.q: Before what did Ekalavya practice archery in the forest?
options: A sacred fire he kept burning
questions.explanation: Ekalavya fashioned a clay image of Drona and practiced before it with such devotion that he attained near-unmatched skill.

questions.q: What did Drona finally demand from Ekalavya as his teacher's fee?
options: A vow never to fight Arjuna
questions.explanation: Drona claimed Ekalavya's right thumb as gurudakshina, the one price that would cripple his archery.

questions.q: How did Ekalavya respond to Drona's demand for his thumb?
options: He begged for a lesser price
He challenged Drona to a duel
He cut it off without hesitation
questions.explanation: With a cheerful face and no hesitation, Ekalavya severed his right thumb and offered it to Drona.

questions.q: Which animal's barking mouth did Ekalavya famously seal with arrows?
questions.explanation: Ekalavya filled the mouth of a barking dog with arrows so deftly that the animal was silenced without being wounded.

questions.q: How many arrows did Ekalavya shoot into the barking dog's mouth?
questions.explanation: Ekalavya loosed seven arrows into the dog's open mouth, the feat that revealed his skill to the Kuru princes.

questions.q: Whose supremacy as an archer did Ekalavya's sacrifice ultimately protect?
questions.explanation: By surrendering his thumb, Ekalavya gave up his superiority so that Arjuna would remain the world's foremost archer.

questions.q: In which art did Ekalavya achieve near-unmatched mastery through self-practice?
questions.explanation: Through years of solitary devotion, Ekalavya made himself an archer to rival the best of Drona's own pupils.

questions.q: What did the feat with the barking dog demonstrate about Ekalavya?
questions.explanation: Sealing the dog's mouth without harming it showed astonishing lightness of hand, and is often described as shooting guided by sound alone.

questions.q: The dog whose mouth Ekalavya sealed had wandered from whose party?
questions.explanation: The dog had strayed from the Pandavas, who were out hunting in the forest, and its return with a mouth full of arrows astonished them.

questions.q: Which prince was most shaken on witnessing Ekalavya's skill?
questions.explanation: Arjuna was deeply troubled, for the forest archer's skill threatened the supremacy Drona had promised him.

questions.q: What promise of Drona's lay behind the demand for Ekalavya's thumb?
options: That a Nishada would never hold a bow
That Ashwatthama would inherit his lore
That no pupil would surpass Arjuna
That all kings would honor the Kurus
questions.explanation: Drona had assured Arjuna that no pupil of his would ever excel him, and the thumb was the price of keeping that word.

questions.q: How did Ekalavya shoot after losing his thumb?
options: He gave up the bow forever
With a wooden thumb he carved
questions.explanation: Ekalavya continued to shoot using his remaining fingers, still formidable but no longer supreme.

questions.q: How did Ekalavya receive Drona when the master arrived at his forest range?
options: With reverence, honoring him as his guru
With a challenge to test him
With gifts of gold and game
questions.explanation: Ekalavya touched Drona's feet and honored him as his teacher, offering him whatever he wished.

questions.q: What is Ekalavya's sacrifice most often held up as an emblem of?
questions.explanation: Ekalavya's unquestioning gift of his thumb is the epic's starkest image of guru-bhakti, though it is also widely read as the cost of caste exclusion.

questions.q: Where did Ekalavya carry out his years of archery practice?
options: In a cave in the Himalayas
At a hermitage he made in the forest
In the courtyard of Drona's home
questions.explanation: After Drona's refusal, Ekalavya withdrew to the forest, set up the clay image, and trained there in solitude.

questions.q: When the astonished princes asked who he was, how did Ekalavya identify himself?
options: A wandering hunter with no master
A pupil of Parashurama in hiding
A king come to challenge Drona
The Nishada prince and a disciple of Drona
questions.explanation: Ekalavya named himself the son of the Nishada chief and declared himself a disciple of Drona laboring at mastery of the bow.

questions.q: What custom did Drona invoke when he asked Ekalavya for a fee?
questions.explanation: Drona invoked gurudakshina, the customary offering a pupil owes his teacher, before naming the thumb as his price.

questions.q: What became of the dog after Ekalavya's seven arrows?
options: It returned unhurt to the Pandavas, its mouth propped open
It fell dead at Ekalavya's feet
It fled deeper into the forest, never found
It led Ekalavya back to Drona's camp
questions.explanation: The dog ran back to the Pandavas unharmed with the arrows filling its mouth, and the sight led them to seek out the archer.

questions.q: Why did Drona travel to Ekalavya's forest range?
options: To recruit him for the Kuru army
To see the archer who claimed to be his pupil
To hunt with the assembled princes
questions.explanation: After Arjuna's anguished complaint, Drona went with him to see for himself the forest archer who called himself Drona's disciple.

questions.q: According to the epic, who eventually slew Ekalavya?
questions.explanation: Krishna himself recalls having slain Ekalavya, counting it among the foes he removed from the Pandavas' path.

questions.q: As a grown ruler, Ekalavya is remembered as an ally of which enemy of Krishna?
questions.explanation: Tradition places the adult Ekalavya among the allies of Jarasandha in his wars against Krishna's Yadavas.

questions.q: How did Krishna justify Ekalavya's death when recounting it to Arjuna?
options: As an obstacle removed for the Pandavas' sake
As punishment for abandoning his guru
As revenge for an insult to the Yadavas
As an accident of battle he regretted
questions.explanation: In the Drona Parva, Krishna lists Ekalavya among the mighty foes slain for the Pandavas' good, saying such men had to fall for their cause to prevail.

questions.q: In which book of the Mahabharata does the story of Ekalavya's thumb appear?
questions.explanation: The episode is told in the Adi Parva, within the account of Drona's training of the Kuru princes.

questions.q: What change did the princes observe in Ekalavya's archery after he gave his thumb?
options: His arrows no longer flew straight
He refused ever to string a bow
His aim improved through harder practice
His former lightness of hand was gone
questions.explanation: The epic notes that when Ekalavya shot thereafter with his fingers, his earlier lightness and speed of hand had left him.

questions.q: In the Harivamsa tradition, what is Ekalavya's surprising birth connection?
options: He was Krishna's cousin, raised by the Nishada chief
He was a son of the god Surya
questions.explanation: In the Harivamsa variant, Ekalavya was born to Devashrava, a brother of Vasudeva, and was brought up among the Nishadas, making him Krishna's cousin.

questions.q: Who was Ekalavya's father?
options: Hiranyadhanus, a chief of the Nishadas
questions.explanation: Ekalavya was the son of Hiranyadhanus, a king of the Nishadas, which is why he is called a Nishada prince.

questions.q: How is Ekalavya described when the Kuru princes first saw him in the forest?
options: In white silk with a golden bow
In ash-smeared robes of a renunciate
In royal armor with a peacock crest
In black deerskin with matted hair
questions.explanation: The epic pictures him dark-bodied, clad in black deerskin, with matted locks, a figure of the wild utterly unlike the palace princes.
